Please consider during reviews:
- the build pipeline status which is the refernce build for the project. If it passes, it means it compiles.
- the module layout indicated in the CMake configuration and the partition layouts indicated in the respective `main` partitions.
- the codecov coverage report.

As external north stars for implementation correctness and terminology go, please consider some of the literature items in the docs/paper/references.bib.

Try to prevent divergence between the textbook literature, the implementation, the doxygen inline documentation, the README and the LaTeX draft paper.

For the doxygen headers, please check that they follow common formats which usually include a brief, a summary of the partition contents, a copyright notice and a pertinent or serendipitous quote from a not very famous practitioner in the field (in their native language and with English translation).

For build checks, it's OK to use the CI build on a draft PR as opposed to building locally. Commits are squashed before a merge to `main` is executed so it does not have to look or perfect prior to pushing to a draft PR. Quite the contrary: the CI build is the authoritative reference build, so check the PR build status on a regular basis.

The flow should be:
1. pick up one or more issues.
2. create branch, push and open a draft PR.
3. iterate on the branch, push on reasonable checkpoints, check the build status every now and then to avoid divergence from the reference build.
   - Before pushing, run `make format` to auto-apply clang-format to all `*.cpp` / `*.cppm` files. The CI "Check Code Formatting" step runs `clang-format-21 --dry-run --Werror` and will fail immediately if formatting is off — this is the fastest check to fix locally.
   - The full CI build takes a few minutes. Poll `gh pr checks <number>` roughly once a minute until all checks are green (no "pending"). If a check fails, fetch logs with `gh run view <run-id> --log-failed`.
   - The codecov coverage report is published asynchronously, roughly one minute after a successful CI build. Check it and address any coverage regressions before flagging the PR ready for review.
4. when the dust is settled, flag the PR as "Ready for Review" with `gh pr ready <number>`.
5. Iterate with reviewer until the dust is settled. The reviewer is a GH Copilot, so when in doubt, ask for a review too many.
   - Request a review with `gh pr review <number> --request` or via the GitHub UI.
   - The review also takes a few minutes. Poll `gh pr view <number> --json reviews` once a minute until a review appears, then address any comments.
6. When all seems within reasonable limits, squash the commits and merge to `main`.
